data.title: Watsco: Gradually Warming Up To This HVAC Player
data.description:
In the fall of 2023, I believed that it was time for shares of Watsco, Inc. (WSO) to cool off. This came as a strong player in a fragmented A/C unit industry had seen its share performance far outpace that of the operating performance. This pushed up earnings multiples to the mid-20s, rapidly deteriorating the risk-reward proposition for prospective investors.

Heading into early 2026, shares have risen by just a couple percent, just over 10% if dividends are included, thereby dramatically lagging the performance of the wider market. This comes as Watsco has been struggling in between sluggish operations in construction as well as new regulations impacting the industry.

By now valuations are still rich but have come in quite a bit recently, as I am curious to learn about anticipated profit growth in 2026 to potentially have a more upbeat stance.

Managing Temperatures

Watsco helps both homeowners and contractors to fix broken and/or failing A/C units in homes and businesses. The company offers both physical and online fulfillment of new equipment and parts, which contractors can order for their respective job sites.

The company operates 700 locations throughout North America, some 90% in the United States, complemented by smaller operations in Canada, Latin America, and the Caribbean. The company benefits from scale in what otherwise is a fragmented industry, as it is looking to provide superior customer service through its online offerings, a segment that it rather rapidly has built up over the past decade or so. The company furthermore attributes its success to having deep ties with OEMs and distributors, a strong company culture, and a strong track record.

With 90% of its $7 billion in sales generated in the United States, the company is largely a residential play, a market that makes up about two-thirds of sales, complemented by smaller new housing and commercial end markets. Most of the sales are tied to HVAC units in residential settings, commercial settings, and other HVAC products.

The U.S. market is large, with an estimated 120 million HVAC units running in the country, the vast majority of which are replacement markets, with nearly 10 million units shipped in 2024. Lower construction activity is certainly not advantageous, but not that much of a detractor, as much is about repair & replacement. By now, the company operates in a $74 billion marketplace, with the business serving no less than 72,000 contractors, installers, and technicians across the board.

With revenues, profits, and shareholder returns compounding at levels in the teens since the 1980s, undoubtedly Watsco is a great long-term value-creating business here. This is certainly the case with regulations getting stricter and climate change increasing the demand for cooling solutions.

More Recent Advancements

A near $200 stock pre-pandemic traded at a high of $380 in the fall of 2023, when I last looked at the stock, even as higher interest rates were causing a damper on some end markets of the business. For the year 2022, the company posted an impressive 16% increase in sales to $7.3 billion, driven by inflation and strong demand coming out of the pandemic.

Operating margins rose to 11.4% of sales, as topline growth and margin gains made it so that earnings rose by more than 40%, with adjusted earnings reported at $15.41 per share, or about a dollar less if we correct for some tax benefits.

Paying out a near $10 per share dividend, which, by the way, was hiked for 49 years in a row, the dividend yield was seen near 3%. So far, the good news is that sales have trended flattish to down slightly so far in 2023. This, together with a 25 times multiple awarded (after those earnings rose 40% in 2022), made me a bit cautious, notably given the significant valuation multiple inflation observed in the stock.

Booming Further, Coming Down

In the end, the conclusions from 2023 were far too conservative, as the stock actually boomed at $550 per share in 2024. What followed was a big setback, and trading near the $500 mark over the past summer, shares hit a low of $325 in the fall of last year and are now trading at $375 per share.

Aided by strong performance in the second half of 2024 and some bolt-on M&A, Watsco grew 2024 sales by nearly 5% to $7.62 billion, yet operating profits in dollar terms were down slightly amidst some margin pressure seen.

GAAP earnings were reported at $13.30 per share, down thirty-seven cents from the year before. The company returned to operate with a net cash position, as a convincing 11% hike in the dividend was announced, now payable at $12.00 per share.

Sales trends were sluggish so far in 2025, with first-quarter sales down 2%, second-quarter sales down 4%, and third-quarter sales down 4%. This is despite some bolt-on M&A activity taking place in the spring, including the acquisition of Southern Ice Equipment Distributors, adding 10 locations and $47 million in annual sales.

With sales slightly down so far this year and margins being largely flat, some minimal pressure on earnings is observed in the first nine months of 2025. More telling is that the company is suffering some dilution, indicating an unwillingness to buy back stock, with the share count up just over 1% over the last year, while the company operates a rock-solid balance sheet.

Year to date, earnings are down forty-four cents to $10.48 per share, as net cash holdings stand at $640 million. In fact, buybacks were discussed on the most recent earnings call, yet management does not seem too eager just yet, even while recognizing that shares came down. Instead, the company prefers to maintain financial flexibility to pursue M&A over buybacks here.

What Now?

Trading at $375 per share, the issue is that earnings power of around $13 per share is actually down a bit from two years ago, amidst tougher operating conditions. Net cash holdings now stand at nearly $17 per share, for an operating asset valuation of $358 per share, resulting in a 27 times earnings multiple.

All this is a bit too easy here, as 2025 is not really representative. In the midst of a tougher housing market, Watsco is even more impacted by new regulatory rules for new HVAC systems incorporating so-called A2L refrigerants. That actually impacted half of Watsco's sales and over a billion in inventory in a transition that ran between September 2024 and some point early in 2026. This created additional handling, volatility, and complications, all weighing on sales and margins, on top of tough market conditions already.

Hence, there is a real roadmap for 2026 earnings to advance. Also, as investment into the business peaked in 2025 outside the A2L implications, the degree of which is not yet shared. Hence, I am getting more interested in Watsco here, but in between unknown earnings growth seen in 2026, I am erring on the side of caution, but I am keen to keep a closer eye on the shares into the year.

data.title: My Ultimate Dividend ETF Combo For 5%+ Yield And 10%+ Annual Growth
data.description:
Combining attractive yield with double-digit annualized growth is exceptionally rare in today’s market. This is because while the market is not perfectly efficient, it is not unintelligent either. Therefore, when something is expected to grow at a strong clip, its yield is typically very low. And when it is not likely to grow at a brisk pace at all, the market often prices it such that its earnings yield and potential dividend yield are quite high.

Thus, investors are often forced to pick either high yield with no growth or high growth with little to no yield, or potentially combine the two for a mix of both, or even find individual stocks that have moderate growth and a moderate yield. However, there are three ETFs in particular in today’s market that offer an attractive current yield and have a track record of growing their dividends at a double-digit annualized rate.

The three funds that I’m going to discuss in this article have yields that range between 3% and 8%, and they have dividend CAGR histories over the past 3 to 10 years of 10% plus. Moreover, together they also provide pretty decent sector diversification and geographic diversification, and put together they deliver a pretty attractive yield and dividend growth rate.

The Global Infrastructure ETF Powering Income And Growth

The first ETF I’m going to discuss is the iShares Global Infrastructure ETF (IGF). This fund tracks the S&P Global Infrastructure Index and focuses on infrastructure operators rather than infrastructure builders. The fund operates in a passive rules-based manner, and in so doing can keep its costs down.

Its portfolio is broken down by roughly 40% utilities (XLU), which bring regulated, highly defensive cash flows to the table, though they also experience substantial interest rate sensitivity due to their bond proxy status. Another 35% to 40% of the portfolio is invested in transportation infrastructure, including airports, toll roads, railroads, and railroads, which typically have inflation-linked revenue streams and provide attractive diversification through their status as mission-critical assets. Finally, the portfolio is rounded out with 15% to 20% exposure to energy infrastructure (MLPX), including pipelines, LNG, and storage. These businesses tend to also have contractual fee-based earning streams that are dictated by volumes rather than commodity prices, which makes them pretty defensive as well.

The fund, on the whole, has 97 holdings at the moment, though it does have pretty high conviction in its top 10 positions, with them totaling nearly 40% of the fund’s capital. The fund also invests heavily in developed markets in order to sustain regulatory stability in its infrastructure assets, which helps to keep risks down. On a trailing 12-month basis, it yields 3.2%, which is relatively attractive given that it’s nearly three times what the S&P 500 (SPY) offers today.

Moreover, its recent dividend growth track record is phenomenal, with a five-year dividend CAGR of 14% and a three-year dividend CAGR of about 17%, which also indicates the dividend growth has been accelerating in recent years. On top of that, its expense ratio of 0.39% is quite modest, given the unique selection of stocks from all over the world that might be more difficult and even expensive for an individual investor to try to replicate.

A Diversified Dividend Growth Machine Built For The Long-Term

The next fund we’re going to discuss is the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). This fund also has a little over 100 blue-chip dividend growth stocks in its portfolio. It places a primary focus on quality, sustainability, consistent dividend growth, and current yield in selecting those holdings. It provides broad diversification across the energy (XLE), healthcare (XLV), consumer goods (XLP), industrials (XLI), and financial sectors (XLF), while also having a little bit of exposure to technology (XLK).

Given the heavy focus on healthcare and consumer goods names, it is a bit more defensive by design, and its lack of exposure to infrastructure and international names means that it complements IGF nicely. It also has a bit higher yield with a trailing 12-month dividend yield of over 3.7%, while its dividend CAGR over the past decade has been 10.6%, and it has a 13-year dividend growth streak, which makes it a powerful dividend growth machine, along with the yield that’s a bit higher than IGF’s.

Moreover, it has an extremely low expense ratio of 0.06%, making it a very efficient fund. While it has underperformed in recent years as its focus on energy and consumer and industrial stocks has weighed on it due to inflationary pressures, tariff disruptions, and energy price weakness, it still has a lot going for it moving forward. For example, its low fees, its long-term track record of solid total return performance, its stellar dividend growth, and its attractive valuation today given its high yield and its concentration in sectors that have been beaten down in recent years makes it look like an attractive income play with total return upside potential if valuations normalize and the market rotates back toward traditional dividend growth stocks.

The High-Yield Backbone Most Investors Overlook

The third and final ETF we’re going to discuss today is the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP). This fund brings in the major source of yield for a portfolio that would be made up of these three funds, as it has a trailing 12-month yield of 8.2%. Moreover, as a midstream MLP-focused fund, it enjoys long-duration contracted and inflation-protected cash flows that round out the infrastructure provided by IGF alongside the more broadly diversified portfolio that SCHD holds.

Additionally, it has excellent dividend growth recently, with a three-year dividend CAGR of 10.3%, which makes it an exceptional combination of yield and dividend growth. It is also nice in the sense that it gives investors exposure to leading MLPs like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), MPLX (MPLX), and Energy Transfer (ET) that would otherwise each issue K-1 tax forms, and instead investors only have to deal with a 1099 tax form.

That being said, there is somewhat of a loss of benefits as the ETF itself is taxed as a C corporation, and it also charges a 0.85% expense ratio. Still, it can be a valuable component of an ETF-focused portfolio or even simply a portfolio that wants MLP exposure without the K-1.

Investor Takeaway

All three of these funds, even if they do not keep up their 10% plus dividend per share CAGR in each case over the long term, still enjoy powerful growth tailwinds, as SCHD is built with dividend growth machines that have relatively low payout ratios in many cases and proven growth models. Meanwhile, IGF and AMLP should benefit from major infrastructure growth tailwinds ranging from AI-driven energy demand, which should boost IGF’s utilities and energy holdings as well as AMLP’s well-located energy infrastructure in the United States, as well as booming demand for infrastructure across the world that should drive strong demand for IGF’s transportation and utilities assets.

When mixed together, these funds should be able to deliver a mid-single-digit yield in the 5% range (depending on the exact weighting assigned to each fund) and deliver growth that will crush inflation over time, thereby enabling investors’ purchasing power to grow over time. These ETFs also generally hold businesses that are defensive in nature and are likely to prove to be durable during a period of disruption from AI as well.

As a result, they are three of the very best ETFs a dividend growth investor could find - especially if they prioritize a balance between current yield and long-term dividend growth - and reflect many of the traits that I seek to find when I pick individual stocks to achieve even higher yield, lower beta, and greater long-term total returns in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Consumer Price Index: Inflation At 2.7% In December, As Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Inflation cooled for a second straight month in December as the headline figure for the Consumer Price Index came in at 2.68% year-over-year. This is down from 2.74% in November and was consistent with the forecast. Additionally, the core CPI was at 2.64% year-over-year, up slightly from 2.63% in November and lower than the forecast of 2.7%.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices were up 0.3%, as expected, while core prices were up 0.2% versus the projected 0.3% monthly increase.

Here is the introduction from the BLS summary, which leads with the seasonally adjusted monthly data:

The Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) increased 0.3 percent on a seasonally adjusted basis in December, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Over the last 12 months, the all items index increased 2.7 percent before seasonal adjustment.

The index for shelter rose 0.4 percent in December and was the largest factor in the all items monthly increase. The food index increased 0.7 percent over the month as did the food at home index and the food away from home index. The index for energy rose 0.3 percent in December.

The index for all items less food and energy rose 0.2 percent in December. Indexes that increased over the month include recreation, airline fares, medical care, apparel, personal care, and education. The indexes for communication, used cars and trucks, and household furnishings and operations were among the major indexes that decreased in December.

The all items index rose 2.7 percent for the 12 months ending December, the same increase as over the 12 months ending November. The all items less food and energy index rose 2.6 percent over the last 12 months. The energy index increased 2.3 percent for the 12 months ending December. The food index increased 3.1 percent over the last year.

The first chart is an overlay of headline CPI and core CPI (excludes food and energy) since the turn of the century. The highlighted two percent level is the Federal Reserve's target inflation rate.

In December, price pressures eased from 2.74% in November to 2.68%, the lowest level since June. Meanwhile, core prices inched up from 2.63% in November to 2.64% in December.

The next chart shows both series since 1957, the year the government first began tracking core inflation. In recent years, we have seen some of the highest inflation rates since the second of the two recessions in the early 1980s.

Consumer Price Index Components

The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) divides all expenditures into eight categories and assigns a relative size to each, which is shown in the chart below. The BLS weighs these annually, with the latest weighting taking place in December 2024.

Here is a table showing the annualized change in Headline and Core CPI, not seasonally adjusted, for each of the past six months. Also included are the eight components of Headline CPI and a separate entry for Energy, which is a collection of sub-indexes in Housing and Transportation.

We can make some inferences about how inflation is impacting our personal expenses depending on our relative exposure to the individual components. Some of us have higher transportation costs, others medical costs, etc.

A conspicuous feature in the year-over-year table is the volatility in energy, significantly a result of gasoline prices, which is also reflected in Transportation.

Here is the same table as above with month-over-month numbers (not seasonally adjusted).

Inflation: Fed's 2% Target

The Fed is on record as having a target rate of 2% for core inflation. The Fed traditionally uses the PCE Price Index as their preferred inflation gauge; however, the Consumer Price Index is another common measure.

The COVID-19 pandemic helped launch inflation into its highest levels since the 1980s. As a result, the Fed has been battling high inflation over the past few years with its monetary policy. Inflation has eased off of its 2022 highs; however, the back half of 2024 and most of 2025 have shown just how sticky it can be.

In their latest meeting, the Fed implemented a widely anticipated 25 basis point cut to the federal funds rate (FFR), bringing it to a range of 3.50%-3.75%. This is the third consecutive rate cut and puts the central bank’s range at its lowest level since November 2022.

The statement from the meeting revealed the Committee believes "inflation has moved up since earlier in the year and remains somewhat elevated," but that they are strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective.

The Fed will meet again at the end of January, where they are expected to hold rates steady. At the time of writing, the CME FedWatch Tool shows a 97% likelihood the Fed will hold rates where they are versus a 3% likelihood of a 25 basis point reduction.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ArcelorMittal: Outperformance Confirmed 2025, 2026E Is Coming (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In this article, I'll be updating my longer-term thesis on ArcelorMittal S.A. (MT). I bought and expanded my position in the company in 2025, even going so far as upgrading my target for the company. Since that time, the company has significantly outperformed the already-good returns of the broader index. As such, my investment is one of several in 2025 that have significantly outperformed the broader market. Also, in this case, as with Norsk Hydro (NHYDY), the company was neither an insignificant nor a small one in terms of the percentage of my total portfolio.

So the 50%+ RoR, compared to an 11% RoR from the SPY since August, is significant and demands my update and potential action in the case of a thesis change or overvaluation. You can find that previous article here, and see the RoR below.

This company is in the multinational steel industry. When it comes to commodity companies, such as metals & mining, I prefer the multinationals over the nationals for investing. And in the case of ArcelorMittal, I've also gone ahead and invested in one of the more significant ones. ArcelorMittal is one of the leading companies in this field in the entire world, with an almost 20-year history that combined Arcelor and Mittal Steel, with operations in over 55 countries with end markets across engineering, industrials, and others. In the spirit of de-commoditization for its operations, its latest pushes have involved focusing on things like "smart steel," low-carbon steel (again, like Hydro for aluminum), as well as vertical integration. The vertical integration in this particular case means that ArcelorMittal does everything from ore to advanced steel.

As with other corporations in similar or ancillary sectors in mining, the company's results and operations saw resilient trends, with 3Q25 generating a beat despite challenging environments and markets. This is also reflected in the company's solid share price performance - up 50% and more, as you can see above.

So what remains here, and is it time to "leave" the company behind at this time?

That's what I'll be covering in this article.

ArcelorMittal Q3 '25 results spell out potential further upside, but only at a higher multiple

If you were only to look at the company's current share price relative to my previous target, you'd be excused for expecting me to change my rating here. My PT for MT was $45/share, and it's now at close to $47/share, which is a significant difference. It's also worth noting, as I did in my previous piece, that ArcelorMittal is notorious for not hitting targets or forecasts accurately.

The upside that I previously saw at $32/share is now mostly gone, and the company has almost normalized, or is now reflecting close to all of the upside that I saw apparent in my last analysis.

What I still like in the company, and what I believe might not yet be reflected in the current valuation, offering potential for a PT raise, is the company's exposure to growth markets. Operating in its 55+ countries, MT has a relatively unique access, at least if you couple it or consider it with its asset base.

I want to make it clear before going forward, that this article represents fulfillment of the thesis I invested at. If I chose to sell off every share I had in the company, I would call this investment a very good success - beating the market by a factor of over 4x in less than half a year. It's these sorts of investments that I focus on at this time, shorter-term "in and out," while tolerating longer holding periods if necessary, for safe and good dividend-carrying companies.

However, there's a potential for a price target, or PT, raise here, that's why I'm making this piece a bit longer and more than just "target achieved - moving on."

3Q25 saw results of $1.6B in EBITDA, with a good shipment volume.

But that was expected. The current forecast for 2025E in the US ADR MT is an AEPS growth of almost 165%, and that is now already, I believe, included in the share price. here. At current valuations, more upside would be dependent on growth above that coming for the full year in 2025E. What this could come from is something that ArcelorMittal has been presenting for some time. India is one source - one of the things I like about MT is its relatively and comparatively small exposure to China. The company's focus on growth markets in India as well as parts of Africa is a very positive one. We can't take away from the very volatile trends, growth, and years of underperformance for this company, but we can moderate it somewhat.

The danger and risk is that even good operations fail to combat the overall high-level trends here. The high-level trends of which I speak are related to overcapacity from Chinese steel producers, strong pushes for decarbonization (necessitating significant investment levels), while navigating trade, pricing, and other things. This is a very demanding situation for any producer, including ArcelorMittal. The biggest issue that I'm seeing is the sheer output that cheap Chinese producers are currently delivering. This is what's creating the currently unfavorable position and pricing for global steel, unless you're behind curtains of protectionism, such as the EU TRQ quota system, or anti-dumping measures, which have introduced a 62%+ stamp duty on imports of steel from China.

Even this might not be enough, however, and in any case, ArcelorMittal is active on more markets where it directly competes with Chinese steel products, irrespective of EU duties. While countries like India also implement anti-dumping duties with specific targets of China and Vietnam, they are nowhere near the level of what the EU does.

It's therefore fair to say that the volatility in the sector is for a reason, and any surge, such as the one that we've seen in ArcelorMittal, needs to be seen in the context of what we can expect from the risk side. The one thing I can see that most nations seem to agree on is that domestic/international steel manufacturers cite state-subsidized Chinese producers of the metal as a problem for domestic and transnational profitability.

While this might not be as much of an issue for ArcelorMittal's non-commoditized production, it is an issue for the broader market and thus impacts company valuation, regardless of solid Q3 '25 results. The company may leverage technology, integration, diversification, and quality to combat these things, but the problem remains.

To clarify it beyond doubt, the problem that I see in global steel (not just me, other analysts as well), is the demand for decarbonization and ESG while at the same time competing with low-cost state-subsidized producers of what is arguably lower quality steel from China (Some manufacturers don't follow standards llike ASTM, boron additions, or but, it's obviously an individual thing, with some Chinese products being solid), which impacts profit and pricing. If these trends move in the wrong direction, the result is negative AEPS/profitability, and as we've seen in the past, such trends can persist for many years.

So while Q3 '25 was a solid quarter, it doesn't change my perception of the higher risk, which we are at this particular time.

Let's look at what this does to valuation.

Valuation for ArcelorMittal - Upside is now slimmer

As you can see above, part of the reason for the company's outperformance (though hard to say exactly how much) is also buybacks. The danger of buyback-induced returns or upsides is that they can disguise how much of an actual upside there is. The current global macro for the steel sector points to a bit of a recovery, driven by industries not necessarily related to automotive, but to things like infrastructure.

The issue is that this doesn't really take away from the ongoing trends of protectionism and other risks. That means that my overall view for the sector and for ArcelorMittal, in the light of a 50% higher valuation, is not a positive one.

I went back and forth as to whether to increase my PT here, but I could not get away from that an overall increase in PT would only come at a cost of significantly higher risk, when what I invested in at $32/share as of my last article was a cheap/low-valuation, high-quality steel producer with a superb overall upside.

Because that is no longer the case, and because forecasts for the 2026E period and beyond, at best, call for a modest growth or stabilization of both revenue and EBITDA, especially when you consider the company's track record in terms of forecast accuracy, I don't feel like I have a choice except to downgrade my rating for the company.

There is a lot to like about ArcelorMittal - unfortunately, after a 50%+ RoR, which is an outperformance not even I expected during this short a timeframe, the things to like are far less than they once were. I maintain my PT and my fair value from my last article.

Alternatives to ArcelorMittal at this time are businesses like Nucor (NUE), which have also seen outperformance, but may have bigger and better upsides due to more concentration of revenues. Similar steel peers exist in most geographies, but after the past 20 years, I would argue that most of the major steel producers have some sort of "profile" that they work with - general commoditized steel producers or metal companies are getting more and more rare.

It's fair to say that the general state of the steel industry following the last 6-7 months is one of less appeal than the time before, due to intense outperformance. As such, I now view ArcelorMittal as a rotation potential and may decide to rotate my position in the company.

Risks & Considerations

Tariff risks are still by far the largest to consider as of this quarter. It's also not an invalid argument to say, even now, that we need to wait for the full impacts to be able to be calculated. While we've seen some realization of some of the risks, there's a potential for more to come.

Secondary risks, due also to its EU exposure, are is lack of ESG/sustainability development compared to other producers. ArcelorMittal is still mostly legacy, unlike companies like SSAB (as well as other producers not strictly in steel), and the cost to shift this, if this becomes too costly, will be massive.

Beyond that, we're mostly talking historical correlations, such as MT being a historical underperformer, and as of this valuation, it no longer really rises above this, different from my last article.

My updated PT remains, therefore $45/share for the ADR MT, but one that is less attractive than before.

Thesis

My updated thesis for ArcelorMittal is currently neutral. I view the company as no longer being all that investable on the basis of its recent outperformance and potential downside into 2026E, following Q3 '25.

The company's recent performance details not only operational improvements and good upside based on reversal, as well as new growth projects/JVs, but also higher upside due to a high book value, thanks to share buybacks. These share buybacks are, after a 50%+ performance, now part of the risk of the company, and these good results do not mean that this company is attractive despite what I view as a solid potential here.

Instead, I update ArcelorMittal to Hold, and I may rotate my position in the company here. My PT for MT is upgraded to $45/share, with the company currently trading at just below $47/share as of January 2026.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is no longer enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of $45/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a Hold rating as of January 2026.

data.title: TD Synnex: Setup Remains Very Attractive
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about TD Synnex Corp. (SNX) previously with a buy rating, as the Hyve growth engine was accelerating, the core business was moving up the value chain, and the new growth tailwinds were also helping to drive growth. Execution has exceeded my expectations. Fast forward to today, SNX continues to grow nicely, margins have held up, and the balance sheet has strengthened. Adj. EPS should continue as I expected, which should push up valuation, making the upside even more attractive than I thought.

Q4 2025 earnings

SNX’s Q4 2025 results confirmed my bullish view on the business. Revenue was up 10% y/y to $17.4 billion, beating consensus and also coming in above the top end of management’s guidance. The growth was broad-based, with Endpoint Solutions delivering 10% billings growth, showing that the PC refresh cycle I noted earlier was still ongoing, and Advanced Solutions up 13% y/y, driven by Hyve and data center infrastructure. Solid growth drove margins up across the board. Gross margin was up ~30 bps y/y to 6.87%, driven by rising average selling prices [ASP] across PCs, servers, and storage. Higher gross flowed down to the EBIT line, driving an expansion of ~20 bps. Overall, adj. EPS came in at $3.83, ahead of both consensus and guidance.

Accelerating Growth and Resilient Demand

The way I framed Hyve was that it has turned into a real growth driver for SNX, but I am very surprised (positively) that it is doing way better than expected. Demand is actually accelerating, with gross billings in Q4 up >50% y/y, a sharp jump from what we saw in Q3 (up mid-30% y/y). This effectively confirms the fact that hyperscaler spending remains very robust and that the cloud/AI build-out is still in an aggressive phase. And we can cross-verify these against the comments made by the hyperscalers. For instance, Microsoft (MSFT) explicitly said the $34.9 billion quarterly capex is related to growing demand for cloud and AI, with spending going heavily into GPUs/CPUs and datacenter sites. They also said that demand again exceeded supply even as they brought more capacity online, which implies their ramp in spending should continue. Google (GOOGL) also raised capex again to meet AI-driven demand, and so had Amazon (AMZN), which noted they’ll continue to be very aggressive investing in capacity because they see demand and that it’s still quite early.

All these suggest the demand side of the equation remains very strong, and the fact that SNX was able to capture this growing demand suggests that Hyve’s original design manufacturer business model is resonating with customers better than expected. For the hyperscalers, they are not just buying individual components off-the-shelf to get their data centers up and running. They are buying full-scale systems that can be deployed quickly to get their AI workloads up and running, and hence, dealing with one scaled vendor that can handle everything makes their life easier.

That said, this magnitude of growth, then, raises a question: how sustainable is it? History has taught us that hardware sales are cyclical and usually flip negative when input costs go up too much. The one hardware that has most of the market attention is memory prices. The logic is straightforward: higher component costs would very likely push customers to delay orders or cut investment budgets (reduce scope of need). However, SNX has proven it is not heavily impacted. Management did acknowledge the rising prices but, importantly, noted limited evidence of pull-forwards, cancellations, or demand destruction. In other words, this tells us that customers are absorbing the higher costs and proceeding with their investments anyway.

This is huge and very bullish because it speaks well of the demand quality. It instills more confidence that demands arising from data center/AI IT infrastructure spending are structural, which makes the demand runway more sustainable.

Addressing the Bear Case

Previously, the one major risk I flagged was SNX’s balance sheet. I was worried about Hyve growth that requires significant working capital for rapid expansion. The idea was that inventory has to be funded before cash comes back in, and that dynamic can really cause a liquidity drag if the demand/cash flow timing is not well managed. But I think the Q4 2025 results have put this bear case to rest.

Instead of being a drag, SNX free cash flow [FCF] came in at ~$1.42 billion in Q4, up significantly vs. the ~$512 million in Q4 2024 and also ~7x more than Q3 2025. This tells us a few things. Firstly, Hyve’s growth is not a drag on working capital. Secondly, management has tight control of working capital even as volumes scale.

This also flips the bear case into a very bullish one. If Hyve can continue to grow at this rate, which I don’t think is impossible, FCF could potentially be sustained at a >$1 billion rate (on an annual basis). And if that is true, the implications for stock sentiment are meaningful. One, lower liquidity risk means lower risk premium (resulting in a higher multiple). Two, SNX has more cash to return to shareholders. As of Q4, SNX has more than $2.4 billion of cash, which can easily support its growth capex (~$150 million/year so far) for multiple years. This also means SNX could potentially allocate all of its >$1 billion FCF for buybacks. Take $1 billion as the baseline; that is ~8% yield (vs. ~$12 billion market cap today).

Valuation

I upgraded my share price target to $229.5 with a total return expectation of 55% over the next two years. I believe SNX can continue to compound EPS at the rate I expected (12% y/y growth), driven by organic earnings growth and share buybacks (could be 8%/year easily). But I expect SNX to trade at a higher multiple (12.5x forward PE vs. the previous assumption of 11.5x) since CDW Corp (CDW) trades at 12.5x with only high-single-digit earnings growth expectation. This gets me to a share price target of $229.5. Rolling forward my estimates to FY28, I have also included consensus DPS estimates for FY26/27 into my total return calculation.

Risk

While execution so far has been flawless, the implied deceleration for 2H 2026 is something to watch out for. Management’s full-year guide suggests growth moderating to mid-single digits, and that could create a narrative headwind (although I think investors would just look forward as demand is still strong). The rising memory costs are also a potential margin headwind. Although demand has been inelastic so far, if memory prices continue to surge, the higher prices could cause some enterprises to pull back on spending.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating on SNX. I believe the equity story given that Hyve is growing faster than expected and solid free cash flow performance has removed the balance sheet risk. With earnings growth, cash generation, and capital returns on the table, I expect SNX to continue compounding adj. EPS at a low-teens percentage rate. Valuation should inch higher given where CDW is, and overall, I think the current setup remains attractive for investors.

data.title: Alphatec Holdings: Backing Improves
data.description:
Over the summer, I concluded that Alphatec Holdings, Inc. (ATEC) was delivering on continued growth while narrowing losses. While this was to be applauded, I feared persistent losses and continued dilution as a key risk to investors.

Focus on growth of the business had come at the expense of shareholder value, amidst debt taken on and dilution taking place. While innovation and growth were seen, true profitability remained elusive, and despite a reasonable 3 times sales multiple, I erred on the side of caution.

This was followed by strong momentum in the second half of 2025. Following a soft, or conservative, outlook for 2026, a small setback has been observed here. With my appreciation for Alphatec growing, current valuations are not compelling enough to buy the dip just yet, as I am keen to keep a closer eye on the shares here.

All About The Spine

Alphatec aims to revolutionize spine surgery by creating clinical distinction and driving surgeon adoption, all designed to gain a greater market share of an already growing market. Part of its mission is not just to provide better spine solutions, but also to address its greatest challenge, that of lack of durability.

The company specifically cites a competitive advantage versus larger and more diversified global medical peers like Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Medtronic (MDT) and Globus Medical (GMED) by being purely focused on the spine.

The company focuses on a wide range of products used for pre-intra and post-operations. Products include imaging, implants, and monitoring, used in a wide variety of procedures and approaches, all supported by the EOS insight platform of the business.

Alphatec went public at $9 per share in 2006, yet that does not tell much given a massive reverse stock split taking place ever since, with shares going public at >$100 per share if we factor that in. Shares fell to the $1 mark in the 2010s as a $100 million business at the time was posting substantial losses.

A complete strategic overhaul to focus on growth, focus, and margin components sent shares trading up to the $15 mark in 2021-2024. However, towards the end of 2024, shares were back to just $5 per share before momentum reappeared in 2025.

Managing Growth

The strategic reset made 2022 sales rise in a spectacular fashion, reported up 44% to $351 million. This, however, was accompanied by a huge operating loss of $147 million.

Revenues rose by another 37% to $482 million in 2023, as operating losses rose further to $173 million, even as modest gains have been seen on a relative basis here.

Revenues rose by another 27% to $612 million in 2024, as operating losses narrowed to $136 million, with some real progress on the bottom line made, yet still large losses being reported.

With some stagnation seen on the margin front in the first quarter of 2025, shares traded at $11 over the summer, with 146 million shares valuing equity at $1.6 billion. This, however, excluded about half a billion in net debt load, for a $2.1 billion enterprise valuation, equal to about 3 times sales guidance at $734 million for the year.

Having been skeptical of Alphatec in various cases before, I acknowledged that some trends were turning positive, including positive cash flows, although these are not the same as profits, among others, adjusted for quite heavy stock-based compensation being doled out.

It was more the willingness and ease with which management was willing to dilute shareholders that made me cautious and detrimental down the road.

Making Progress

Since the summer, shares have made real inroads, doubling to $23 towards year-end 2025, as a setback early in 2026 set shares back to the $18 mark, still marking massive returns since the summer.

Second-quarter sales rose by 28% to more than $185 million, as GAAP operating losses narrowed to $13 million, marking $22 million in gains on that front, with those numbers even impacted by amortization charges and some smaller litigation charges as well. Driven by the strong momentum, the full year's sales guidance was hiked by eight million to $742 million.

Third-quarter sales rose by more than 30% to nearly $197 million as GAAP reporting losses ticked up to $16 million, which is an increase on a sequential basis, while marking a dramatic improvement versus the year before. The sequential reduction came after litigation & amortization charges rose quickly to over $10 million this quarter. If adjusted for, shows that realistic operating losses are rapidly approaching the breakeven mark. That, however, is all ahead of taxes and substantial cash expenses due on a nearly half-a-billion net debt load.

The company hiked the full-year sales guidance to $760 million, suggesting that fourth-quarter revenues are seen around $209 million. This marks further substantial growth, which is frankly impressive. The good news is that dilution is taking place at a slower pace, with the third quarter share count up just over 5% on the year before. While this obviously is not great, yet amidst 30% topline sales growth, revenue growth on a per-share basis is impressive, while net debt is pretty flat.

Driven by these very impressive numbers, shares have been re-rated higher to nearly $23 towards year-end, now having pulled back to $18 per share.

Starting 2026 Soft

Shares of Alphatec suffered a setback at the start of 2026 as the company provided preliminary numbers and issued its 2026 outlook. Fourth-quarter revenues are seen near $213 million, all a bit stronger than originally guided for. So far, so good.

The issue was more seen with the 2026 outlook, calling for about 17% sales growth, with revenues seen around $890 million. Adjusted EBITDA is seen around $130 million, which compares to a $91 million number on this front in 2025, and while this marks further progress, it is a bit less than investors anticipated after expectations ran hot.

With 151 million shares trading at $18, the market value of Alphatec has risen to just over $2.7 billion, for a near $3.2 billion enterprise valuation. Based on the $764 million in sales expected in 2025, the business trades just over 4 times sales now. Note that these EBITDA numbers are entirely different from realistic earnings.

Stock-based compensation expenses trend at $70 million here, while cash and non-cash interest expenses trend over $40 million. Furthermore, depreciation expenses trend at $60 million each year as well. This makes it so that realistic earnings are not yet seen, even as cash flow positive numbers are reported; those disregard stock-based compensation expenses and dilution, of course.

And Now?

Having been skeptical of Alphatec in various cases in the past, I am impressed with continued growth and continued operating leverage achieved, although realistic earnings remain elusive here. In between all this, valuation looks pretty reasonable at 4 times sales, given that these sales grow by 30%, yet a slower pace of growth in 2026 and lack of realistic earnings remain an issue as well.

Weighing it altogether, I am more constructive on Alphatec amidst continued growth and operational leverage, yet I am not yet willing to alter my stance entirely. Alphatec remains a neutral play for me, and while my appreciation for the business and its growth has improved meaningfully, I am not buying this latest dip just yet.

data.title: JETS: Don't Underestimate The Long Term Risks Associated With Owning Airline Stocks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

U.S. Global Jets ETF (JETS) aims to provide broad economic exposure to air travel, though with a significant emphasis on U.S. airlines.

While the airline industry is certainly an exciting one, I do not agree with the fund sponsor's thesis that "the global commercial airline industry represents a highly dynamic investment opportunity".

This is because, although I recognize that airlines have recovered from the massive disruption of the pandemic era, I think it is essential that the long-term risks associated with the industry are not overlooked. Given the low margins and weak balance sheets of most airlines, they seem particularly vulnerable to the increasingly uncertain geopolitical environment which has developed over recent years. I therefore give the fund a strong sell rating.

Holding Selection

Before analyzing the ETF, it's essential to understand how it is put together. The ETF is passive in the sense of following a quant-based selection criteria, reflected in an index which was created solely for the fund.

The selection criteria, as shown above, does have a number of distinct layers.

At quarterly rebalances, the fund buys 10% stakes in each of the four leading U.S. airlines, amounting for 40% of the fund in total. It's important to note that "leading" here is defined as a ranking which balances market cap, trading volumes and to a lesser extent passenger load factor (though the specific formula used by the ETF manager to balance these three factors is not public).

In the next layer, the criteria is expanded to include Canadian airlines too, alongside U.S. airlines. The next eight leading names (using the previous definition) are then given a 3% weighting, representing 24% in total.

The next layers down then use a slightly different selection formula, which rather than emphasizing market cap and dollar volumes traded, instead balances "cash flow return on invested capital (CFROIC) and average dollar value traded with additional inputs based on sales per share growth, gross margins, and sales yield". This formula is applied to eight U.S. and Canadian securities with a 2% weight (16% in total). It's also important to note that from this stage down, it's not just passenger airlines which are covered, but rather a broad selection of businesses relating to airlines (e.g. airport operators, booking platforms and aircraft manufacturers).

The final two stages apply these same quantitative metrics to 10 international companies (with 1% ranking each, so 10% in total), then again to 20 further international companies (with 0.5% each, so another 10%).

Given these rules and their overall complexity, it's worth getting a practical sense what the fund currently owns. The top ten holdings below account for around 61% of the fund's total holdings. Notably, resulting from the first "layer" outlined above, around 44% of the fund is represented by the four largest U.S. airlines (though this resets to 40% at each quarterly rebalance).

Holding Name Allocation
Southwest Airlines Co 11.53%
United Airlines Holdings Inc 11.03%
Delta Air Lines Inc 10.64%
American Airlines Group Inc 10.64%
Allegiant Travel Co 3.19%
Sun Country Airlines Holdings Inc 3.04%
JetBlue Airways Corp 2.91%
Air Canada 2.88%
Alaska Air Group Inc 2.84%
SkyWest Inc 2.68%

While the bulk of investor returns are clearly linked to the results of the big four airlines shown above (Southwest, United, Delta, and American), it's worth understanding the broad categories owned overall.

Based on the full list of holdings available on the fund's webpage, I have compiled a table showing the major categories covered:

Category Percentage Share Number
U.S. Airlines 61.02% 10
Canadian Airlines 2.88% 1
International Airlines 11.63% 19
Airport Operators 3.47% 6
Booking Platforms 7.35% 5
Aircraft/Component Manufacturers 10.04% 6
Other 3.30% 3

While there is a degree of diversification, investors should understand that JETS is overwhelmingly a bet on passenger airlines, currently accounting for more than 75% of the total holdings. It is also a substantial bet on U.S. airlines specifically, since these represent 61% of the fund's value.

Challenging The Bull Case

The fund's investment case gives a number of reasons why investors should have a positive long-term outlook on the airline industry.

One of the reasons stated is that

As economies around the world develop, they generate increasing numbers of middle-class consumers with disposable incomes. This trend is particularly strong in emerging markets such as India and China, where consumers are more likely to spend money on experiences such as travel.

While it is certainly true to an extent, it does seem a stretch to apply this secular tailwind to the JETS fund specifically. This is because, from my point of view, it is not clear that the U.S. airlines which dominate the fund will be able to monetize the middle classes of India and China. While the fund has 0.55% stakes in two Chinese airlines (China Southern and Air China), these seem very unlikely to materially move the needle for the fund's performance.

Another reason which I do not find the investment case convincing is because it overlooks the low margins and weak balance sheets of airlines, which make them particularly vulnerable to geopolitical shocks. For example, we can consider the big four U.S. airlines emphasized in the fund.

Tangible Book Value (millions) Net income margin Levered FCF Margin
American ($10,091) 1.11% 0.78%
Delta $3,101 7.36% 3.27%
Southwest $6,511 1.38% -0.05%
United $7,120 5.64% 3.91%

Particularly notable, and accounting for over 10% of JETS is American Airlines which enjoys net profit margins of just over 1% and has a negative tangible book value of around $10 billion. This weakness is also reflected in its B+ credit rating from Fitch, far into the "junk" bond category formally described as "highly speculative". While the bonds of Delta and Southwest are at least at an investment grade according to the ratings agencies, United's bonds are not considered non-investment grade either, with a BB+ rating from Fitch. This is far from encouraging for long-term investors.

Exogenous Shocks

While airline stocks can perform reasonably well during stable periods, the real risk comes from extended periods of disruption such as we saw in 2020-21.

As shown above, the JETS ETF was even able to outperform the S&P (since inception) until early 2018. As a result of the pandemic, though, the ultimate divergence in performance is pretty stunning. Overall, the S&P offered a total return almost 11 times greater than JETS during the 11 years from inception.

I believe that the market is underpricing the combination of the weak financial profiles of airlines as well as the relatively high probability of disruption to their operations over the next decade. Indeed, even if only considering pandemics, the probability of a serious one taking place over the next 25 years has been estimated at around 50%. Scientists have also suggested that the next pandemic could be worse than COVID. The effect of this on the broad airline industry would likely be catastrophic, as already evidenced by the fund's performance post-COVID.

Fees

Another negative consideration is the fund's relatively high fee burden, with an expense ratio of 0.6%. For long-term holders, this results in a substantial loss of capital to fees. For example, over a ten year holding period, around 5.8% of total return is lost to fees. Over a twenty year period, the figure is 11.3%. It's also important to consider that, given the high fee ratio, almost the entirety of the fund's ~0.8% dividend yield is consumed by these charges.

Upside Risks

Clearly, there is the possibility that my perspective on the industry is excessively negative. In the short-term at least, the back-log in terms of deliveries of commercial aircraft is a positive consideration for passenger airlines, since there is less supply to serve the ever-increasing demand for travel.

In the long-term, however, my serious concerns remain, and I do think this outweighs any short-term positives which could occur, such as a decline in oil prices or artificial intelligence allowing operators to become more efficient and expand their margins.

Conclusion

Modern passenger aircraft are a clear technological marvel. Unfortunately though, the business of operating them is much less marvelous, demonstrated by the razor-thin margins of the four largest U.S. airlines which represent the core focus of the JETS fund. In consideration of the significant possibility of sudden and unpredictable disruption to the airline industry in the coming decades, I consider them to be unsuitable for long-term investors, and am compelled to award a strong sell rating to the ETF.

data.title: Visa's Growth Is Great, But Doesn't Make It A Buy
data.description:
When it comes to the payment processing industry, one company that absolutely should not be ignored is Visa Inc. (V). The firm is a giant in the space and has a great track record of growth. Unfortunately, the market has reflected that in the company's share price, with the stock often trading at rather lofty multiples. But for those who want a high-quality company that should do reasonably well in the long run, this is certainly a prospect to keep in mind. In my last article about it, published in September of 2025, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘hold’ candidate. This was based on the overall quality of the firm and its growth. Since then, the stock has outperformed the market slightly, going up 6.2% compared to the 5% rise that the S&P 500 experienced. Having said that, since I originally rated it a ‘hold’ way back in November of 2023, the stock has underperformed some, rising by 42.8% compared to the 52.4% that the market experienced.

Despite this long-term underperformance, this is one of those plays that, I believe, makes sense for those who want stability and safety. The overall growth of the company should continue, especially with recent innovations that management has made. And the overall fundamental health of the company remains intact. Given these factors, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ candidate makes the most sense to me.

A Great Business

Over the years, I have come to view Visa as a great business. But there is a big difference between a company being great and its share price making sense. The fact of the matter is that the company is great because financial performance continues to chug along nicely. Take the most recent quarter as an example. This is the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company was $10.72 billion. That's 11.5% above the $9.62 billion the company reported a year earlier.

The company has benefited from multiple drivers during that time. For instance, the number of cards that it has outstanding hit 4.9 billion for that quarter. That's up materially from the 4.6 billion that it had outstanding the previous year. In fact, even though it was modest, the company saw an increase in market share associated with cards. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean. This particular chart looks at market share amongst three big players in the industry, with those being Visa, Mastercard (MA), and American Express (AXP). Market share inched up ever so slightly from 56.32% to 56.40%. I know that's not much of an increase. But any sort of growth in a large, mature, and hyper-competitive space, is great to see.

There are other metrics that we should be looking at also. For instance, year over year, overall payments volume for the company increased about 9%. But this requires some unpacking. According to the data provided, the company processed $4.375 trillion worth of transactions during the quarter. That's up from the $4.249 trillion processed the previous year. The growth in the number of cards certainly helped to facilitate this. By comparison, in its latest quarter, Mastercard was only responsible for $2.747 trillion. American Express was far behind, totaling $479.2 billion. Unfortunately, because of the more rapid growth experienced by the competition, specifically with Mastercard experiencing an increase of 9.8% year over year, Visa’s share of transaction value contracted from 59.08% to 57.56%. But it still is the big player amongst the three.

Transaction data is also valuable for us to assess. Because this increase in processed volume was not just from customers spending more. It was actually from an increase in the number of transactions. This makes sense considering the rise in the number of cards outstanding. The company processed a mind-boggling 82.1 billion transactions for the quarter. That's up from the 79.3 billion transactions reported the previous year. And for 2025 in its entirety, the 272 billion transactions of the company processed ended up coming in 8.1% above the 251.5 billion reported for 2024.

As revenue for the company increases, you would normally expect profitability to rise. As the chart above illustrates, this was the case for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. But as the first chart in this article shows, profits can be somewhat lumpy. In fact, net income dropped from $5.32 billion in the final quarter of 2024 to $5.09 billion the same time of 2025. The good news is that this drop was driven primarily by $903 million in litigation costs that the company incurred. This compares to just $10 million reported the previous year. Unfortunately, litigation has become rather expensive for the company as of late, but in theory it should be a one-time thing. For 2025 in its entirety, the company reported litigation charges of $2.56 billion. That's massively above the $462 million reported a year earlier.

This rise in costs was driven by additional accruals of $2.2 billion that the company incurred in order to address claims involving the interchange multidistrict litigation situation that it has going on. This is essentially an antitrust case that the company is contending with, whereby it has been alleged that it and competitor Mastercard illegally fixed interchange fees and restricted competition. The company has long-faced these charges. In fact, even though it was partially overturned, back in 2012, a $7.25 billion settlement was reached regarding these types of claims. And in 2024, a judge rejected a $30 billion settlement offer. But this is still an ongoing issue, with some estimates pegging the ultimate nominal value of this litigation to be $38 billion.

Despite these problems, the company is still doing quite well. In the final quarter, it is true that operating cash flow of $6.24 billion fell short of the $6.66 billion reported the previous year. But when we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $9.32 billion to $9.62 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business totaled $7.37 billion for the quarter, which was up from the $6.63 billion the company reported the previous year.

If we use the historical results that the company achieved for 2024 and 2025, we can see in the chart below how the company is priced compared to the same two competitors that I stacked it up against earlier in this article. Although the trading multiples for Visa are rather lofty, they are in line with the competition. The fact of the matter is that the payment processing industry has a tendency to trade at high multiples anyways. So to see the company at least come in and around the same levels as these other firms is encouraging in and of itself.

Long term, I do believe in the operational opportunities that the company offers. Management continues to come out with positive developments and good news. On December 17th of last year, for instance, the company announced a new strategic collaboration with Akamai Technologies (AKAM), which is a cybersecurity and cloud computing company, whereby the firms will tap into the power of agentic commerce. For context, this refers to AI software agents in the dew shopping on behalf of people. This includes things like browsing, comparing items, and even purchasing products using little to no human involvement. It is essentially an AI shopping assistant. This particular arrangement will involve Visa’s Trusted Agent Protocol, which is used to authenticate AI agents and link them back to the real consumers that they represent. If done correctly, this will empower merchants to trust and distinguish legitimate AI agents from malicious bots before any transaction is ultimately completed.

Also in December, the company launched stablecoin settlement in the US. This is an opportunity that I discussed about for the company in a previous article. In essence, the company mentioned that it was launching its USDC settlement in the US, which would give issuers the benefit of faster funds movement over blockchains, including across weekends and holidays. This is a big opportunity for the company, and management has long been working on growing into this market. Already as of November 30th, the company accomplished monthly stablecoin settlement volume in excess of $3.5 billion on an annual run rate basis. So it is already doing well in this industry. Literally, a day before that, on December 15th, management announced the launch of its Stablecoin Advisory Practice, which is a new value added service provided by the business that will provide actionable insights and recommendations for banks, financial technology firms, merchants, and other businesses, when it comes to things like market fit, strategy, and implementation in the stablecoin space. Seeing as how the stablecoin industry is already worth more than $250 billion and is certain to grow further, this could be an attractive and high margin revenue source for the company.

There have been other updates as well that fall outside of business development and innovation. On December 23rd of last year, for instance, the company revealed data regarding holiday sales. They said that spending rose 4.2% year over year, with e-commerce leading the way with a 7.8% jump. Surprisingly, electronics were an area of growth for consumers, with spending up 5.8% year over year. Although e-commerce sales continue to grow, according to management, 73% of all holiday payment volume in the US is still in physical stores. I honestly find that number hard to believe. Also, it's worth noting that other countries saw strength as well, with spending up 5% in Australia, 4.4% in Canada, 7.9% in South Africa, and 3.6% in the UK. All of this bodes well for the company, considering its business model.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that Visa is a fantastic company. Long term, I expect it to continue growing. But this does not mean that it makes sense to buy into. The stock is rather lofty, and looks more or less fairly valued compared to other firms in the space. And even though I expect continued business development and innovation initiatives to bode well for the firm, I firmly believe that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ candidate is the right choice.

data.title: BYD Is A Key Part Of The World Shifting Towards BEVs
data.description:
Introduction

Per my August article, BYD Company Limited (BYDDY) is seeing growth in terms of selling new energy vehicles (“NEVs”) overseas. This continues as the December CPCA National Passenger Market Report says BYD exported 131,637 units in December. Unlike Tesla (TSLA), who only sells battery electric vehicles (“BEVs”), BYD also sells plug-in hybrid vehicles (“PHEVs”). NEVs include both BEVs and PHEVs.

Per the December National Passenger Market Report, China accounts for 68% of global NEV share for the first 11 months of 2025. This includes 64.3% of the BEV share and 76.4% of the PHEV share. As such, if we want to analyze the global NEV market then we have to look at China and if we want to analyze China’s NEV market then we have to look at BYD.

BYD saw BEV sales climb almost 28% from 1,764,992 units in 2024 up to 2,256,714 units in 2025 per the December CPCA Manufacturer Ranking Report. It's impressive how they sold 491,722 more BEVs in a year when sentiment in some markets was bearish on BEVs.

My thesis is that BYD is a key part of the world shifting to BEVs.

At the time of this writing, RMB 1,000 is about $143.32.

The Numbers

On a global basis, Tesla was slightly ahead of BYD with BEVs in 2024 but everything changed in 2025. It was a year where BYD sold nearly half a million more BEVs worldwide while Tesla saw a drop of more than 150 thousand units. Combined, BYD and Geely (GELYY) sold nearly a million more BEVs in 2025 than they did in 2024 while Tesla saw units fall:

Despite headlines about flat BEV markets in parts of the U.S. and Europe, BEV sales have been increasing substantially worldwide and much of this is because of BYD and China. Per the January 2025 CPCA National Passenger Market Report, global auto sales for 2024 were 90.6 million units and BEVs had 11.4% share while PHEVs had 6.3% share implying unit numbers of 10.3 million and 5.7 million, respectively. For 2025 global units, we only have the first 11 months at this time from the December 2025 CPCA National Passenger Market Report. For the first 11 months of 2025, global auto sales were 87.66 million. The global BEV penetration rate rose significantly to 15.3% while the PHEV penetration rate went up slightly to 7.9% which implies 11-month unit figures of 13.4 million and 6.9 million, respectively. In just the first 11 months of 2025, the global BEV unit sub total beat the full year total for 2024 by over 3 million units!

BYD is by far the largest BEV wholesaler in China but many other OEMs are copying some of their techniques and increasing their numbers. Above we showed the global units for Tesla but below we only show only the Tesla China wholesale units:

1.5%

XPeng BEV NEV

429,445

1.5%

Xiaomi NEV

411,837

1.4%

3,745,056

12.7%

1,866,255

6.3%

1,336,800

4.5%

858,963
