GWM ICE

737,857

2.5%

SAIC-GM-Wuling ICE

255,809

Other ICE

9,179,316

31.1%

Total

29,554,000

99.1%

The above numbers are from the wholesale tables of the December CPCA Manufacturer Ranking Report where we have the top 10 overall OEMs and the top 10 NEV OEMs. We can get some of the ICE leaders by taking the overall leaders and subtracting their NEV units which gives us their net ICE units. But BYD is the #1 overall OEM and they have no ICE units.

Additionally, Tesla China is the #10 overall OEM and they don't have any ICE units either. FAW-VW, SAIC VW and SAIC are in the top 10 overall and they have large ICE sales but I don't have their year-end NEV unit sales so I don't have their precise net ICE figures. The ICE number above for Great Wall Motor (“GWM”) comes from their overall number in the wholesale table of the December CPCA Manufacturer Ranking Report less their 403,653 NEV number from their December HKEX filing. Li Auto (LI) and GAC Aion also have considerable NEV sales but they are not broken out above as they did not finish in the top 10 NEV OEM wholesaler rankings for 2025.

Leapmotor had a 2025 YoY unit growth rate of 104.7% as their extended range electric vehicle (“EREV”) NEVs did well.

One of the reasons BYD and Geely sell so many BEV units is because of the low price point for some of their top selling models. Neither company had a strong December as they faced complications with government incentive programs and tremendous competition in China. XPeng (XPEV) also has a low-priced model, the Mona M03, and it is one of the reasons they had tremendous YoY unit growth of 125.9% in 2025.

Driver Assistance And Autonomy

Driver assistance and autonomy require the combination of the prodigious battery power and the electrical architecture found in BEVs. These are obvious selling points in the decade ahead and a quote from Lloyd Lee’s recent Business Insider article illustrates this idea:

Tesla's Full Self-Driving feature was the primary reason I made the purchase. I would say the electric aspect is almost an inconvenience, and FSD is my main interest in the Tesla.

Huawei is making waves in China with their Harmony Intelligent Mobility Alliance (“HIMA”) network. Per CnEVPost, there were 589,107 units delivered under HIMA in 2025. The biggest part of this is the Seres AITO and we know Seres had 430,282 wholesale units in 2025. The Chery Luxeed is also a considerable part of the network and Chery says this model had sales volume of 90,493 units in 2025. The HIMA network is a competitive threat to BYD as it allows small OEMs to enjoy economies of scale by tapping into the network.

Xiaomi (XIACY) is another fierce competitor in China. Tapping into their smartphone ecosystem, they integrate their cars with their other products and they had a 2025 YoY unit growth rate of 200.9%. Their vehicles have long waiting lists and their 2026 units are bound to be higher than what we saw in 2025 as they continue to figure out production challenges.

BYD’s smart driving system is called God’s Eye. A January CarNewsChina article says BYD has a team of more than 5,000 people working on autonomy but this is a small fraction of their employee headcount which was listed as 968.9 thousand in the 2024 annual report.

Valuation

Forward-looking investors should keep tabs on driver assistance progress. This can be more important than some of the unit numbers - especially units for low-priced models. Robotics is another area where investors need to check updates in 2026.

I’m excited about BYD being the global leader with BEVs but the automobile business is tough and U.S. investors face serious risks when allocating capital to Chinese companies. Additionally, China’s economy has come into question recently. It is important to separate positive sentiment about the BEV unit numbers from the reality of the stock prospects.

Looking at the Q3 '25 report, the 2024 annual filing and the Q3 '24 report, trailing twelve month (“TTM”) net profit to shareholders is 9M25 + FY24 - 9M24 or RMB 38,349 million or RMB 23,333 million + RMB 40,254 million - RMB 25,238 million. This comes from TTM revenue of RMB 841,117 million or RMB 566,266 million + RMB 777,102 million - RMB 502,251 million. I wouldn’t argue with sanguine investors who have an optimistic valuation range of up to 20 to 25x the TTM net profit to shareholders of RMB 38,349 million, giving us a range of RMB 766,980 million to RMB 958,725 million which is about $110 to $137 billion.

The Q3 '25 report says the following about the share count:

In July 2025, the Company issued 2,431,252,684 bonus shares and 3,646,879,026 shares by way of capitalization of capital reserve, upon which, the number of ordinary shares was increased to 9,117,197,565 shares. Earnings per share for the periods presented was calculated based on the adjusted number of shares.

Before July 2025, one BYDDY ADR was equivalent to two regular shares but this is no longer the case. It is now a 1:1 ratio with the H shares. As such, we multiply the 9,117,197,565 shares times the January 12 BYDDY ADR price of $12.44 to get a market cap of $113.4 billion. The market cap is close to an optimistic valuation range and I think the stock is a hold for long-term investors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NVDY: Holding NAV While Paying High Yields
data.description:
YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NVDY) is a high‑yield, single‑stock options‑income synthetic covered call ETF that has Nvidia (NVDA) as the underlying stock it writes options against.

For those new to this type of ETF, synthetic covered calls mean it doesn't own any of the stock of Nvidia, but rather utilizes options for the purpose of generating income for the investor. It also means the upside is capped while fully participating in any downside moves of Nvidia.

The ETF uses Treasuries and cash as collateral for the options. And as already mentioned, its primary purpose is to generate income.

Since the fallout from tariffs, which found a bottom in April 2025, the ETF has actually held up very well, more than holding NAV while maintaining a high distribution yield.

In this article, we'll look at the pros and cons of holding NVDY, and what the future potentially holds for it.

The Underlying

With a single‑stock options‑income ETF like NVDY, the most important thing to consider in regard to starting or adding to a position is the underlying stock itself, which in this case is Nvidia. How one views the potential future performance of Nvidia is what should determine any decision.

The major thing I want to mention there is the sentiment concerning AI, which some think has entered into bubble territory. We don't know if that is true yet, but even if it is at the beginning of a correction, it doesn't change the demand for Nvidia's products over the next year or so. Spending by current and potential new customers will be the key there.

My thought is that there is probably more room to run for Nvidia for at least the remainder of 2026, with the caveat there could be a correction during that time from profit taking or something in the news cycle that panics shareholders. Even under that scenario, I think it would recover in a relatively short time, until there is proof showing demand is sustainably slowing.

Also, I think it's a mistake to put Nvidia in with the other Magnificent Seven stocks, because of it, being a picks and shovels play for much of the AI sector. The major point is, as it relates to NVDY, an investor's conviction on NVDA's performance is what will drive the results of the ETF going forward. If there is a sustainable and prolonged change to negative sentiment for NVDA, NVDY will drop in price in conjunction with the decline in the share price of NVDA, if that's how it was to play out.

Performance Since April

After the ETF bottomed out in April 2025 at $12.46 because of the impact of tariff declarations from President Trump, it has performed very well for a synthetic covered call ETF. From April, it started a nice upward climb that culminated in reaching approximately $18.00 per share, before it reversed direction. As I write, it is trading at $14.45 per share.

That's important to take into consideration because there are those that assert that covered call ETFs like NVDY always lose NAV. This is proof it doesn't. That doesn't mean it won't in the future, but it's significant to note that while it's throwing off strong distributions, it's able to maintain NAV.

That of course doesn't help those much that got in early when the ETF was trading above $30.00. At that time, starting in March 2024, it paid out $2.62 per share, and paid out distributions between $1.02 and higher through the end of 2024.

So even though there is some NAV erosion there, the distributions were much higher than they are today, which offset a lot of the unrealized losses from the decline in share price, assuming investors held on.

Since NVDA was moving up while the share price of NVDY was falling, it confirms the income from options was keeping up with the monthly payouts at the time (it has since changed to weekly payouts).

In regard to 2025, the performance of NVDY has been much stronger, able to move in alignment with NVDA. Looking at the chart from April 2025 onward, this is actually a strong chart for a synthetic covered call ETF like NVDY, that pays out the high yields it does.

Risks

The risks associated with NVDY are several, including whether or not it'll continue to generate enough income from the options in order to continue to pay out robust distributions.

Another risk is if NVDA experiences the type of correction that is deep and prolonged. Under that scenario, an investor would get hit, not only with a loss from the drop in its share price, but it would also force the fund managers to lower the amount paid out weekly.

As mentioned above, this type of ETF is capped in relationship to the recovery of the underlying. So, after a correction from NVDA, if it recovers, NVDY is unlikely to recover to the same degree. A major thing to think about in that scenario is whether or not the drop in the price of NVDY comes from the price movement of NVDA alone, or the result of NAV erosion. With the former, the ETF will recover some, to the point, I believe that it can regain NAV. On the other hand, if the decline is from NAV erosion, or the combination of both, it'll struggle more to regain its former share price.

The other risk is if and when the share price comes under pressure, it would result in the distribution being lowered.

Finally, in regard to risks, NVDY should be part of an overall income generating portfolio in order to spread the risk. If it's a standalone holding, it's far more susceptible to risk than if it is part of an overall portfolio built to generate income. In other words, single-stock concentration is very risky.

Strengths

When looking at the risks, some may conclude that it's better not to take a position at all in NVDY. I think that would be the wrong conclusion to draw for investors seeking income.

The major strength of NVDY is the high level of income it's able to generate while maintaining NAV since April 2025. Not only that, but for those not using the income for expenditures or other reasons, can take the revenue and reinvest it in other stocks or similar ETFs, which would leverage the distributions into further gains.

Even though the upside in NVDY is somewhat limited, the reason for investing in it should be for income only. I've seen some growth investors talk down high-yield ETFs, but they are not created at all for growth, but for income. To use growth as a metric isn't even relevant to the performance of NVDY.

That's not to suggest that NAV isn't important, only that the goal isn't to significantly grow NAV, but to throw off income. To me, if NVDY is moderately losing NAV, holding NAV, or slightly above NAV, that's exactly how it's supposed to perform.

Last, the key metric is total returns for NVDY. I own the ETF, and am moderately down with NAV, but more than making up for it with the high distributions paid out, i.e., I'm significantly up at this time.

Conclusion

An ETF like NVDY is actively managed, and I think investors need to watch it closely for signs it may underperform. The underperformance would primarily come from the underlying, which would bring about a drop in share price along with shrinking distributions.

Something else to consider is investment goals, strategies, and current age. That will determine the decisions to be made if holding NVDY.

NVDY was created to cater to investors seeking income on a consistent basis. But that income comes with some tradeoffs, including the capped upside, volatility, and full exposure to the downside of the underlying.

For that reason, position sizing and dollar-cost averaging will help mitigate downside risk.

Those that understand how synthetic covered calls work and desire income, should do well with NVDY, taking into consideration the need to keep an eye on it if a major correction comes to NVDA.

As it stands today, the ETF has been very resilient, and if NVDA continues to do well, it should be a solid income generator with little or no NAV erosion.
